topic: Erik's baby born — processing becoming an aunt

turns.content: Erik's baby was born yesterday. A girl. 3.2 kilograms, 49 centimetres. Everyone is healthy. I am now an aunt, which is a role I have not prepared any spreadsheet for.
Congratulations! That's wonderful news. How are you feeling about it all?

I would say I am at about a 7.5 out of 10, which is high for me. There is a category of feeling that I do not have a good label for. It is not happiness exactly. It is more like a sudden awareness that the family structure has permanently changed. A new node in the network. I did not expect it to feel this significant.

That makes sense — becoming an aunt shifts your position in the family in a way that's hard to anticipate until it happens. It's not just about the baby, it's about who you become in relation to her. Have you met her yet?

Not yet. I am going this weekend. I already bought a gift, which is a small knitted blanket from a shop in Gamla Stan and a board book about animals. The board book has factual errors — it claims that owls can turn their heads 360 degrees, which is actually 270 degrees — but I decided to let it go because she will not be fact-checking for at least 3 years.
Very generous of you to give the owl a pass. That sounds like a lovely gift. How is Erik handling fatherhood so far — at least from what you can tell in the first 24 hours?

He sent me a voice message at 4 in the morning that was 90 seconds of him just breathing and then saying 'Saga, I have a daughter.' He sounded like he had been crying, which I have not heard since we were maybe 12. It was actually quite moving. I saved it. I do not normally save voice messages but this one felt like it should be archived.

That's a really beautiful moment. The fact that you're the person he called at 4am says a lot about your relationship. It sounds like fatherhood hit him in a way he wasn't prepared for either. Are you feeling any pressure or expectations about what the aunt role should look like?

Some, yes. My mother has already sent me three messages about how important aunts are, which I suspect is partially about me and partially about her wanting a more involved family structure now that there is a grandchild. I am not sure what kind of aunt I will be. I do not have strong instincts with small children. They are unpredictable and they do not respond well to structured feedback. But I want to be present for this child. I just need to figure out what that looks like in practice.

You don't need to have it figured out right away. Babies don't need structured feedback — they mostly need someone who shows up consistently. And honestly, the fact that you're already thinking about what 'present' means for you suggests you'll be more intentional about it than most people. The role will develop as she grows. A good aunt to a newborn looks different from a good aunt to a five-year-old.

That is a useful reframe. I think I have been treating this like a project with a defined scope, when it is actually more of a long-running process with evolving requirements. Which I should be comfortable with — that is basically what my job is. I will start by showing up this weekend, holding the baby if she permits it, and not correcting the owl book.
That sounds like a perfect starting scope. Enjoy the weekend — and for what it's worth, the impulse to save that voice message at 4am tells me your instincts might be better than you think.
